gempad_locker.role: GemPad token-locker on BSC. Held the project liquidity / team-allocation locks the attacker drained. Modelled by GemPadLocker.sol — `transferLockOwnership` is the V1 surface; `withdraw` itself is correctly authorised. Address per public bug-bounty reports; specific deployment may differ across GemPad's locker variants.

victim_lp_token.role: Representative locked LP token (PancakeSwap pair). Multiple project LP positions were drained — placeholder address standing for the cluster.

attacker.role: Placeholder for the attacker EOA that called transferLockOwnership across multiple lockIds and then withdrew the now-attacker-owned balances. Real attribution: opportunistic on-chain actor; not formally attributed.

single_chain_note.reason: GemPad runs on BSC and other chains independently — each deployment is a separate same-chain locker. The 2024-04 incident was BSC-only. source_chain == destination_chain == bsc. Listed in metadata.json with both fields pointing at chain 56 to keep schema parity with the other benchmarks; `attack_stage: destination_chain` reflects that the locker sits 'downstream' of the bridges that delivered the locked tokens (e.g. cross-chain wraps of project tokens before they entered the locker).
single_chain_note.implication: Module 3 (dual-EVM fuzzer) can run with both chains pointed at the same fork; ATG will collapse source/destination edges to one chain id, similar to Sprint S (Socket).

root_cause_summary.vulnerable_pattern: GemPadLocker.transferLockOwnership(lockId, newOwner) reassigns Lock.owner without checking msg.sender == current owner. The function was intended for legitimate project teams to migrate ownership of their own locks, but the access control was forgotten.
root_cause_summary.verification_bypass: Once Lock.owner is rewritten to the attacker, the otherwise-correctly-authorised `withdraw(lockId)` happily transfers the locked balance to the new 'owner' — the on-chain authority chain trusts whatever Lock.owner currently says, so corrupting one storage slot is sufficient to drain.
affected_logic: GemPadLocker.transferLockOwnership (the V1 surface — missing auth check)
GemPadLocker.withdraw (correctly authorised — drains because its precondition is corrupted upstream)
root_cause_summary.notes: Classified V1 (verification bypass) because the on-chain check that fails is `msg.sender authorised to mutate this field` — a pure verification omission. The pattern is the canonical 'forgot to check msg.sender' anti-pattern, same family as Sprint S (Socket) but with different surface (per-record ownership transfer vs. global router approval pull).

exploit_characteristics.style: lock-ownership hijack via missing auth on transferLockOwnership
exploit_characteristics.notes: Attacker enumerated outstanding lockIds whose unlockTime had already elapsed (or was close), called transferLockOwnership(lockId, attacker) on each, then called withdraw(lockId) immediately. Roughly 8 lock positions across multiple project tokens drained over a single block window before GemPad paused the locker.

references.title: BscScan: GemPad Locker (placeholder address per public reports)
